metadata.description: D3.js force-directed graph: CPS threat actors, MITRE ICS techniques, CVEs, Purdue levels, and targeted sectors

nodes.label: Sandworm
nodes.description: Russian GRU Unit 74455, responsible for Ukraine power grid attacks and Industroyer

nodes.label: TEMP.Veles
nodes.description: Developed and deployed TRITON/TRISIS malware targeting Schneider Electric Triconex SIS

nodes.label: Volt Typhoon
nodes.description: Chinese MSS-linked group pre-positioning in US critical infrastructure for conflict scenarios

nodes.label: APT33
nodes.description: Iranian group targeting oil and gas, aviation, and energy sector ICS environments

nodes.label: APT41
nodes.description: Chinese dual-purpose actor combining state espionage with financially-motivated attacks on industrial sectors

nodes.label: Lazarus Group
nodes.description: North Korean RGB-affiliated group with demonstrated ICS reconnaissance capability and nuclear sector targeting

nodes.label: Berserk Bear
nodes.description: Russian FSB group conducting persistent long-term reconnaissance of Western energy infrastructure

nodes.label: APT34
nodes.description: Iranian MOIS group targeting Middle East oil and gas, government, and financial sectors

nodes.label: T0816: Device Restart/Shutdown
nodes.description: Adversaries may forcibly restart or shutdown devices to disrupt OT operations

nodes.label: T0878: Alarm Suppression
nodes.description: Suppressing or disabling alarms to prevent operator awareness of attack in progress

nodes.label: T0831: Manipulation of Control
nodes.description: Adversaries may manipulate the control systems to cause physical damage or unsafe conditions

nodes.label: T0855: Unauthorized Command Message
nodes.description: Sending unauthorized commands to field devices to alter physical process state

nodes.label: T0800: Activate Firmware Update Mode
nodes.description: Exploiting firmware update mechanisms to load malicious firmware onto OT devices

nodes.label: T0865: Spearphishing Attachment
nodes.description: Targeted phishing with malicious attachments as primary initial access vector into ICS networks

nodes.label: T0817: Drive-by Compromise
nodes.description: Watering hole attacks targeting websites visited by ICS engineers and operators

nodes.label: T0862: Supply Chain Compromise
nodes.description: Compromising ICS vendor software or hardware prior to delivery to the target

nodes.label: T0846: Remote System Discovery
nodes.description: Enumerating network-connected ICS/OT devices to map the operational environment

nodes.label: T0840: Network Connection Enumeration
nodes.description: Identifying communications links between IT and OT network segments

nodes.label: T0801: Monitor Process State
nodes.description: Monitoring sensor values and process telemetry to understand physical operations before attacking

nodes.label: T0853: Scripting
nodes.description: Using scripting languages to automate actions within ICS environments

nodes.label: T0866: Exploitation of Remote Services
nodes.description: Exploiting vulnerabilities in remote access services to move laterally into OT networks

nodes.label: T0807: Command-Line Interface
nodes.description: Using command-line tools native to ICS systems to execute malicious actions

nodes.label: T0856: Spoof Reporting Message
nodes.description: Forging sensor data or status messages to deceive operators and hide ongoing attacks

nodes.label: T0843: Program Download
nodes.description: Uploading malicious programs to PLCs or RTUs to alter control logic

nodes.label: T0835: Manipulate I/O Image
nodes.description: Directly modifying the I/O image in PLC memory to alter physical outputs without changing control logic

nodes.label: T0879: Damage to Property
nodes.description: Intentionally causing physical damage to equipment or facility through cyber means

nodes.label: T0882: Theft of Operational Information
nodes.description: Exfiltrating operational data including engineering files, process diagrams, and ICS configurations

nodes.label: CVE-2010-2568 (Stuxnet LNK)
nodes.description: Windows Shell .lnk zero-day exploited by Stuxnet for USB propagation into air-gapped Siemens STEP 7 environments

nodes.label: CVE-2010-2772 (Stuxnet Siemens)
nodes.description: Hardcoded password vulnerability in Siemens WinCC/Step7 exploited by Stuxnet to access PLC programming environment

nodes.label: CVE-2014-4114 (Sandworm OLE)
nodes.description: Windows OLE remote code execution zero-day used in Sandworm BlackEnergy spearphishing campaigns against Ukrainian energy

nodes.label: CVE-2015-5374 (Siemens S7)
nodes.description: Denial of service vulnerability in Siemens S7 PLCs exploited during Dragonfly/Havex campaigns against energy sector

nodes.label: CVE-2017-9248 (Telerik)
nodes.description: Cryptographic weakness used during TRITON actor initial access phase to reach OT DMZ web servers

nodes.label: CVE-2018-10952 (Schneider Triconex)
nodes.description: Vulnerability in Triconex safety controller exploited by TRITON malware to inject malicious programs into SIS controllers

nodes.label: CVE-2019-13945 (Siemens S7+)
nodes.description: Remote code execution in Siemens S7-1500 PLCs relevant to Industroyer2 targeting of Ukrainian grid SCADA systems

nodes.label: CVE-2022-22965 (Spring4Shell)
nodes.description: Critical Spring Framework RCE exploited to compromise SCADA web interfaces and historian servers

nodes.label: CVE-2023-29298 (Unitronics)
nodes.description: Authentication bypass in Unitronics Vision PLCs exploited by Iranian Cyber Av3ngers against US water utilities in 2023

nodes.label: Level 0: Field Devices
nodes.description: Sensors, actuators, pumps, valves, motors — direct interface with physical processes

nodes.label: Level 1: Control Systems
nodes.description: PLCs, DCS controllers, safety systems — local process control logic

nodes.label: Level 2: Supervisory
nodes.description: HMIs, SCADA, engineering workstations — operator interfaces and process visualization

nodes.label: Level 3: MES/Operations
nodes.description: Manufacturing Execution Systems, operations management, OT DMZ

nodes.label: Level 4: Business/Enterprise
nodes.description: Corporate IT, ERP, business network — IT/OT boundary zone

nodes.label: Energy / Electric Grid
nodes.description: Electric generation, transmission, and distribution infrastructure including grid SCADA and protection systems

nodes.label: Oil & Gas
nodes.description: Upstream exploration/production, midstream pipelines, downstream refining and petrochemical processing

nodes.label: Water & Wastewater
nodes.description: Drinking water treatment and distribution, wastewater collection and treatment systems

nodes.label: Manufacturing
nodes.description: Discrete and process manufacturing including automotive, steel, chemicals, and defense production

nodes.label: Nuclear Facilities
nodes.description: Nuclear power generation, fuel cycle facilities, and research reactors

nodes.label: Transportation
nodes.description: Rail, aviation, maritime port, and pipeline transportation control systems

nodes.label: Chemical Facilities
nodes.description: Chemical manufacturing, storage, and processing with hazardous materials risk

links.description: Sandworm used device restart/shutdown in both 2015 and 2016 Ukraine power grid attacks
Industroyer used alarm suppression to delay operator response during grid attacks
Core technique: Industroyer directly manipulated circuit breaker control logic
Sandworm used spearphishing with malicious Office documents for initial access into energy networks
Industroyer2 and KillDisk used firmware manipulation to brick serial-to-Ethernet converters
Industroyer downloaded malicious programs to grid protection relay controllers
Spoofed SCADA reporting messages to mask attack progress from operators
Primary target: Ukrainian power distribution and transmission operators
NotPetya impacted Ukrainian transport and logistics OT systems
TRITON directly manipulated Triconex safety controllers to inhibit emergency shutdowns
Sent unauthorized commands to Triconex SIS controllers to overwrite safety logic
Safety system alarms suppressed to prevent triggering protective shutdowns
Initial access via spearphishing into corporate network then pivoted to OT
TRITON malware core function: download malicious program to Triconex SIS controller
Goal was to disable safety systems enabling catastrophic physical damage at petrochemical facility
Primary target: Saudi petrochemical facility (Petro Rabigh)
TRITON specifically targeted chemical process safety systems
Used living-off-the-land techniques avoiding signature-based detection in ICS environments
Extensive discovery and enumeration of US critical infrastructure OT/ICS networks
Mapped IT/OT network connections to identify paths to operational systems
Leveraged compromised small office/home office routers and VPN appliances for persistent access
Exfiltrated operational diagrams and network architecture documents from utility operators
Exploited remote access services and VPN appliances to enter OT network segments
Pre-positioned in US electric grid for potential conflict-triggered disruption
Confirmed intrusions into US water and wastewater system control networks
Active in transportation logistics and port management systems
Primary initial access: targeted spearphishing against oil and gas sector employees
Used PowerShell scripting extensively for post-compromise actions in OT environments
Exploited VPN vulnerabilities and remote desktop services to access OT networks
Monitored industrial process data and historian servers to understand operations before potential attack
Exfiltrated engineering files and operational data from energy companies
Primary target: Saudi, US, and South Korean oil and gas companies
Targeted energy sector including electric utilities across Middle East and USA
Supply chain compromises of software updates targeting industrial sector customers
Sophisticated spearphishing campaigns targeting manufacturing sector engineers
Network discovery within manufacturing OT environments to identify valuable systems
Significant industrial IP theft from semiconductor, defense, and pharmaceutical manufacturers
Used command-line tools native to ICS engineering workstations
Primary target: automotive, semiconductor, defense, and pharmaceutical manufacturers
Targeted energy sector for both espionage and potential pre-positioning
Spearphishing targeting nuclear facility staff and energy sector personnel
ICS network enumeration observed in nuclear and energy sector intrusions
Exfiltrated nuclear reactor blueprints and operational manuals from KHNP
Evidence of sensor data spoofing capability in advanced ICS intrusion toolkit
Primary ICS target: South Korean nuclear power operator Korea Hydro and Nuclear Power
Targeting energy infrastructure for both revenue and strategic intelligence
Signature technique: watering hole attacks on energy sector trade websites and vendor portals
Trojaned ICS software and vendor tools distributed to energy sector targets
Extensive OT network mapping at US and European electric utility operators
Mapped connectivity between IT and OT network segments at target utilities
Monitored process state data from historians and SCADA servers without disruption
Collected engineering files, ladder logic, and network diagrams from energy operators
Sole focus: electric utilities and energy infrastructure in US, UK, and Europe
Highly targeted spearphishing against Middle East energy sector organizations
Custom PowerShell and Python-based tools for post-compromise execution in OT-adjacent systems
Exploited remote access services to enter OT network segments
Used WMI and PowerShell command-line interfaces for lateral movement
Primary focus: Gulf state oil and gas operators (Saudi Aramco ecosystem, PDO)
Targeted petrochemical and chemical processing facilities in Middle East
Stuxnet LNK exploit enabled propagation via removable media to air-gapped ICS networks
LNK exploit was delivery mechanism for Stuxnet PLC program download payload
Siemens WinCC password vulnerability enabled Stuxnet to access and modify S7-315/S7-417 PLC logic
Core Stuxnet technique: used WinCC access to download rootkit-modified ladder logic to PLCs
OLE zero-day weaponized in Sandworm spearphishing emails targeting Ukrainian energy companies
Also deployed via drive-by downloads on energy sector websites
Siemens S7 DoS vulnerability used to force device restarts/shutdowns in Dragonfly campaigns
Vulnerability enabled firmware mode manipulation on S7-300/400 PLCs
TRITON actor used Telerik vulnerability to gain initial foothold on OT DMZ web servers
Core TRITON CVE: vulnerability allowed unauthorized command injection into Triconex SIS
Enabled TRITON to download malicious program into Triconex safety controller memory
S7-1500 RCE used for unauthorized PLC program download in Industroyer2-era targeting
Enabled manipulation of Siemens S7-1500 control logic in grid protection relay systems
Spring4Shell exploited against SCADA web front-ends and historian server backends
Unitronics auth bypass enabled Cyber Av3ngers to send unauthorized commands to water treatment PLCs
Used to directly manipulate water treatment control setpoints at US utilities
Device restart/shutdown directly impacts field devices at Level 0
PLC and DCS controller restarts are primary Level 1 impact
Alarm suppression at PLC/DCS level prevents operator notification
HMI and SCADA alarm suppression is primary impact point for operator deception
Physical process manipulation occurs at field device level
Control logic modification at PLC/DCS level causes physical process changes
SCADA setpoint modification can cause downstream physical process changes
Unauthorized commands sent directly to field actuators and safety systems
Commands injected into PLC communication buses
Firmware update mode exploited on PLCs and field controllers
Malicious programs downloaded to remote terminal units at field level
Primary impact: PLC and DCS controller logic replacement
I/O image manipulation directly affects physical outputs at Level 0
PLC memory I/O tables modified to alter physical process behavior
Spearphishing targets corporate email users at business network level
Some phishing directly targets OT operators using MES-connected workstations
Drive-by compromise affects engineer workstations connected to business network
OT DMZ jump servers and engineering workstations targeted
Supply chain compromise initially affects IT software distribution
Trojanized SCADA software or HMI updates directly compromise Level 2
PLC firmware and engineering tool supply chain attacks
SCADA and HMI network scanning from Level 2 perspective
Network discovery primarily conducted from Level 3 OT DMZ foothold
IT/OT boundary mapping conducted from Level 3 network
Enumeration of connections from SCADA to field network segments
Process state data read directly from PLC registers
Primary source: SCADA historians and HMI process data
Scripts executed on SCADA/HMI engineering workstations
Scripting used on MES and OT DMZ servers for lateral movement
Remote service exploitation primary entry point into OT from DMZ
SCADA remote access services exploited for direct Level 2 access
CLI tools on SCADA servers and engineering workstations
Command-line tools on OT DMZ and MES systems
Sensor data spoofed at field device communication level
False process values displayed on HMI to deceive operators
Engineering diagrams and configurations exfiltrated from SCADA systems
Historian databases and operational documentation exfiltrated from Level 3
Physical damage occurs at field device and process equipment level
Control system manipulation can cause physical damage to connected equipment
Initial infection vector via USB at Level 3 engineering workstations
WinCC SCADA compromise via hardcoded password
OLE exploit delivered via email targeting corporate network users
Direct impact on Siemens S7 PLCs at control system level
OT DMZ web server compromise via Telerik vulnerability
Direct exploitation of Triconex SIS controller at Level 1
S7-1500 PLC compromise at control system level
SCADA web interface backend servers at Level 2
Direct Unitronics PLC compromise at Level 1 control system
PLCs control field actuators directly — compromise cascades to Level 0
